ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for pork wonton dry noodles with chilli vinaigrette. Mapped to closest parent 'noodle dish' class (FOODON_03315671) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Noodles, cooked — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for this egg-noodle-based dish with pork wonton and chilli vinaigrette dressing.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for pork wonton dry noodles. A0FJP (cooked noodles) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 ancestor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific prepared dish. SNOMED CT coverage of composite Asian noodle dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker serving of pork wonton dry noodles is approximately 350 g, comprising a portion of tossed egg noodles, three to five pork wontons, and a chilli vinaigrette dressing.

nutrient_source.title: Pork wanton in chilli vinaigrette noodles (dry) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) for the exact dish 'Pork wanton in chilli vinaigrette noodles (dry)', CR ID F05060103. All seven reported nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are taken verbatim from the HPB record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008 — noodles, instant, boiled (GI 52) used as analogous estimate for cooked egg noodles
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pork wonton dry noodles with chilli vinaigrette. GI of 52 is estimated from cooked egg/wheat noodles (GI 52) as documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables of Glycemic Index (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, Diabetes Care 2008;31:2281–2283). A GI of 52 falls in the low GI category (GI ≤ 55). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (52) × carbohydrate per serving (72.275 g for 350 g portion) / 100 = 37.6. The chilli vinaigrette dressing and pork wonton filling do not materially alter the GI of the noodle base.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 317 mg/100 g with minimal potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: A 350 g serving delivers approximately 1110 mg sodium — nearly half the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg — and the soy-based chilli vinaigrette dressing may pool at the bottom of the bowl, increasing actual sodium intake; patients with hypertension should request reduced sauce and add potassium-rich vegetables such as leafy greens or cucumber.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load (GL ~38 per serving) from refined egg noodles with minimal dietary fibre (0.8 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The refined egg noodle base has a low GI (~52) but the large serving size produces a high glycemic load of approximately 38 per portion, which will cause significant postprandial glucose elevation; patients with type 2 diabetes should reduce noodle portion size, add non-starchy vegetables to increase fibre, and avoid additional sweet soy sauce or chilli sauce that contributes further sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (317 mg/100 g), moderate fat (8.7 g/100 g) from chilli oil and pork filling, and refined carbohydrate base
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of borderline-high sodium, moderate fat from pork wonton filling and chilli oil dressing, and a refined carbohydrate noodle base makes this dish a moderate cardiovascular risk; patients with CVD should limit portion size to 200–250 g, request reduced sauce, and pair with fibre-rich vegetables to partially offset the atherogenic dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Arachidonic acid from pork filling and pro-inflammatory refined carbohydrate load
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Pork wonton filling contributes arachidonic acid which amplifies inflammatory mediator production; patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit consumption, monitor individual tolerance to chilli (a nightshade), and consider substituting with a chicken or tofu wonton variant to reduce the arachidonic acid load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork Wonton in Chilli Vinaigrette Noodles (Dry) is a popular Singaporean Chinese hawker dish consisting of springy egg noodles tossed in a spicy, tangy chilli vinaigrette dressing and served with boiled pork wontons (dumplings filled with seasoned minced pork). Unlike soup-based wonton noodles, the dry version is dressed rather than submerged, producing a more concentrated flavour profile with a glossy, lightly oily coating on the noodles. The dressing typically combines chilli oil, black or rice vinegar, light soy sauce, and sesame oil, delivering a balance of heat, acidity, umami, and nuttiness. The pork wontons are soft-skinned with a savoury, slightly fatty filling. Per 100 g, the dish provides 192 kcal, 7.7 g protein, 8.7 g fat, 20.65 g carbohydrate, 0.8 g dietary fibre, and 317 mg sodium. The refined egg noodle base has a low glycemic index (~52) but the typical 350 g serving generates a high glycemic load (~38), making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The sodium content is borderline-high and a full serving approaches half the WHO daily sodium limit. Clinically, this dish is unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes without portion modification, and should be consumed cautiously by those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It is not halal due to the pork content. The dish is a staple of Singapore hawker centres and coffee shops, commonly eaten at breakfast or lunch.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) for the exact dish (CR ID F05060103) at the original confidence of 0.95. The HPB is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (cooked egg/wheat noodles, GI 52) from the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source, and the estimation basis is fully documented.
notes: sugar_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB for this dish; this is treated as confirmed zero per the HPB source record (F05060103) — the chilli vinaigrette dressing does not contain added sugar in the HPB formulation. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) as of 2026-04-16. potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this specific composite hawker dish are not reported in the HPB Foodo database as of 2026-04-16. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on cooked egg/wheat noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). GI 52 falls in the low GI category (GI ≤ 55 per University of Sydney classification). Glycemic load per serving = 52 × (20.65 × 3.5) / 100 = 52 × 72.275 / 100 ≈ 37.6. The dish contains pork and is not halal. The input record listed the condition key as 'diabetes'; this has been updated to 'type2_diabetes' with human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' per Veritas naming rules. Aliases self-check: all strings in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array.
